10.1016/j.jmp.2018.09.001
Visual perception may be viewed as a process of inference: the visual system infers perceptual properties concerning the environment on the basis of partial and uncertain optical information as well as on the basis of its own prior assumptions or tendencies. A central problem in this scientific view is that of “cue integration” — that is, how the visual system efficiently combines the information from distinct stimulus cues, as well as prior assumptions, in order to infer the perceptual properties of the surrounding reality  (Landy, Banks, & Knill, 2011) . Scientists who work in this perspective have some natural expectations about models that are suitable for representing cue integration. They expect, for example, that the more abundant, precise, and consistent the stimulus information for a definite perceptual property – for example, the perceptual slant of a textured surface, which may depend on texture gradients, binocular disparity, and other optical cues – the more precise, stable, and reliable the perceptual property predicted by the model should be. The Bayesian approach to perceptual inference has proven to be successful and has gained popularity in contemporary vision science, and this is due (at least in part) to the fact that Bayesian models (at least those based on the Normal/Gaussian law) have a positive fit with those natural expectations of scientists of visual perception. This paper studies whether that positive fit would be maintained after a move from the mainstream path of Bayesian modeling (based on the Normal law) to alternative paths that may be based on other probability laws.

Recall the following version of the Bayes rule from probability theory:

The stated result is greatly valued in psychological research that adopts the Bayesian approach, because it nicely fits at least three basic theoretical expectations in discussing cue integration ( Battaglia, Kersten, & Schrater, 2011 , pp. 48–50;  Saunders & Chen, 2015 , p. 3).                       which specifies the form, the mean, and the variance of the resulting posterior distribution and allows for appreciations similar to those in the preceding paragraph. This generalization is remarkable, as it indicates that the Bayesian paradigm is apt to represent not only the combination of a prior preference and a stimulus factor, but also – and foremost – the combination of two or more stimulus factors, which may be effective on the same perceptual property. Indeed, the “cue integration” problems to which reference is being made typically concern how the same perceptual property may depend on several stimulus factors (e.g., the apparent distance between two objects may depend on several cues to depth), rather than how coexisting perceptual properties may co-vary with one another (e.g., covariation of apparent size and apparent distance of an object).

The present study originates from this question: Is the stated fit between basic theoretical expectations about cue integration and the implications of Bayesian modeling a  peculiar  feature of models that assume Normal distributions, or is it a  general  feature of Bayesian modeling, in some way independent of the kind of probability law? If it is a feature peculiar to models that assume the Normal law, to what degree do other Bayesian models – that is, models that assume laws other than the Normal one – come close to the ideal performance described above? This analysis focuses on probability laws that issue in families of probability distributions that are “closed under multiplication”. Laws of this kind serve as the focus because they correspond to the so-called “prior families” in ordinary Bayesian models, namely, the families within which the key operation of a Bayesian computation takes place (see the opening definition and the ensuing comments in the next section). Specifically, the subjects of this analysis are the Bernoulli, Geometric, Gamma (including Exponential), Beta, and Pareto laws, which are notable exemplars of parametric families of univariate probability distributions satisfying closure under multiplication.

A preliminary question may be raised concerning the subject of our study: Is it possibly relevant to perceptual psychology? In relation to this discipline, are there plausible reasons for exploring what may be the status of the “desirable properties” of mean and variance within distribution families that are different from the Normal family? True, a large majority of the Bayesian models so far designed in perceptual as well as cognitive psychology have been constructed by presuming Normal functions in key roles within the model. Nevertheless, this introduction presents some arguments for suggesting a positive answer to the preliminary question.

One argument notes that probability laws other than the Normal one actually occur as key components of exemplary Bayesian models in statistical theory. For example, eight out of the eleven paradigmatic kinds of models summarized by  Bernardo and Smith (2000, pp. 436–442)  do involve the Gamma, Beta, and Pareto laws in the role of “conjugate prior family”. It is a fact that the development of Bayesian models in statistical theory often served as a paradigm for the construction of Bayesian models in empirical sciences, including experimental psychology. Thus, at least from a formal perspective, it would not be a surprise to find out that non-Normal probability laws are involved in Bayesian models designed in studying perceptual or more generally cognitive processes.

A second argument begins by noting that the choice of a kind of distributions in designing a model obviously depends on the basic characteristics of the variable  
                         x 
                       to which those distributions are to be associated — in a statistical model, the target variable  
                         x 
                       could be a parameter it is necessary to estimate, while in a model of perceptual psychology, it could be a property in the experienced visual scene or a measure of the perceptual performance. The basic characteristics of a target variable  
                         x 
                       could be such that a direct reference to the Normal law would be definitely out of point, so that a suitable non-Normal kind of distribution should be presumed. For example, the variable  
                         x 
                       could be a  dichotomous  perceptual property — e.g., in suitable stimulus conditions, a rectangular pattern of dots could appear grouped by rows or by columns, a definite piece of surface could be judged concave or convex, and so forth. Of course, it would be inappropriate to associate the Normal law to such a variable that has only two possible states. Instead, by coding the states with the numbers 0 and 1, reference may consistently be made to the Bernoulli law, so that cue integration concerning the dichotomous target property  
                         x 
                       will be represented as a computation within the family of Bernoulli distributions. The likelihood system in the model would simply be a pair  
                         
                            ( 
                            g 
                            
                               ( 
                               y 
                               | 
                               x 
                               = 
                               0 
                               ) 
                            
                            , 
                            g 
                            
                               ( 
                               y 
                               | 
                               x 
                               = 
                               1 
                               ) 
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       of probability distributions (possibly Normal) associated with a stimulus variable  
                         y 
                       suitably defined.

For another example, suppose that the target variable  
                         x 
                       is the  probability  that a definite event is faithfully perceived by an observer — e.g., a given signal is detected. This variable is a quantity having the interval  
                         
                            [ 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ] 
                         
                       as the range of its possible values. The kind of distributions typically associated with such quantities is the Beta law, so that cue integration for inferring the target probability  
                         x 
                       will be mirrored by a computation within the family of Beta distributions. A model could be devised in which the stimulus variable  
                         y 
                       is of Binomial type, on considering the conjugacy relationship that links Beta to Binomial probability distributions ( Bernardo & Smith, 2000 , p. 271). Yet another example may be suggested in which the target variable  
                         x 
                       is a measure of  precision  with which an observer evaluates a definite property in visual scenes — e.g., the velocity of an object in motion. Measures of precision are generally defined as the reciprocals of measures of dispersion – e.g., the reciprocal of variance – and in Bayesian theory, they are typically modeled in terms of Gamma distributions, whose support is the ray  
                         
                            ( 
                            0 
                            , 
                            ∞ 
                            ) 
                         
                       of positive real numbers ( Shiffrin, Lee, Kim, & Wagenmakers, 2008 , p. 1269). Consistently with this use, the integration of stimulus factors that are relevant to the precision measure  
                         x 
                       could be modeled as a computation within the family of Gamma distributions. Stimulus variables  
                         y 
                       involved in the model could be, for example, of Poisson type, on considering that the Gamma family is conjugate with the Poisson distribution family ( Congdon, 2001 , p. 35;  Lee, 2012 , pp. 68–69).

As a third argument, note that some of the non-Normal probability laws to be examined in this paper actually occur as key components of Bayesian models available in perceptual and cognitive psychology. For example, Bernoulli distributions are implicit in the models defined by  Mamassian and Landy  ( 1998 , p. 2823;  2001 , p. 2661) concerning local 3D shape perception, as well as the Bayesian approach to signal detection discussed by  Rouder and Lu (2005, pp. 590–592) . The Exponential and the Inverse Gamma laws, which are a special case and a direct transform of the Gamma law, are involved in the Bayesian models of perceptual inference discussed by  Froyen, Feldman, and Singh (2015, p. 580) ,  Gershman and Niv (2013, p. 6) , and  Knill (1998, p. 1663) . Beta distributions play the role of Bayesian priors for shading contrast in a model of 3D shape perception defined by  Mamassian and Landy (2001, p. 2665) , for light reflectance in a model of lightness perception defined by  Allred and Brainard (2013, p. 3) , and for an “attention weight parameter” in a model of categorization processes designed by  Vanpaemel and Lee (2012, p. 1051) . Pareto distributions – also called “power probability functions” – are components of Bayesian models of perceptual grouping and segmentation defined by  Claessens and Wagemans (2008, p. 4)  and by  Elder and Goldberg (2002, p. 335) . In these and other studies, the cited probability laws do not occur as isolated constructs, in the form in which they will be discussed hereafter. Indeed, they generally occur embedded in complex theoretical constructions. Nevertheless, the mentioned correspondences indicate that the lines of our investigation may really intersect theoretical questions that are implicit in Bayesian modeling within both perceptual and cognitive psychology.

The plan of this paper is the following. Section  2  defines the condition of “closure under multiplication”; remarks that it characterizes the “prior families” within Bayesian models; notes that the Bernoulli, Geometric, Gamma, Beta, and Pareto laws do satisfy that condition; and reminds the readers of the elementary properties of these laws for subsequent reference. The next three sections define “desirable properties” concerning the mean (Section  3 ), the variance (Section  4 ), and the relationship between the mean and the variance (Section  5 ). This is accomplished by diversifying and formalizing the three theoretical expectations noted above in the comment on Eq.  (2) . The validity of these properties is tested for each of the non-Normal probability laws just mentioned. It will be observed that there are conspicuous differences between the laws as regards the validity of these desirable properties. Section  6  summarizes the paper’s findings and comments on their significance for the Bayesian concept of information integration. In specifying the “desirable properties” of mean and variance, perceptual (especially vision) psychology will be referred to preferentially, because this is the discipline that directly motivated the present study. However, the questions raised here and the results of the analyses may be relevant also to other branches of cognitive psychology that are proving successfully interactive with the Bayesian paradigm.

The key operation in a Bayesian computation is the multiplication between two (or more) functions that act on the  same  variable — e.g., the variable

The probability laws we will examine are the Bernoulli, Geometric, Gamma (which includes the Exponential as a special case), Beta, and Pareto laws. We choose these for two reasons. One reason is that all of them result in distribution families that are closed under multiplication (as shown below), so that, as far as formal requirements are concerned, they are suitable for the role of prior families in Bayesian models. Indeed, along with the Normal law, they are among the few conspicuous families of univariate probability distributions that possess the stated closure property. The other reason is that indicated in the Introduction, that is, the actual or potential involvement of the chosen laws in Bayesian models within perceptual and cognitive psychology.

The proof of closure under multiplication of a probability law and the extraction of its update equations may conveniently be performed by acting on a “kernel” of its analytic expression, that is, a part that (possibly) differs from the whole analytic expression by a multiplicative term that is free of the variable ( Bernardo & Smith, 2000 , p. 129). For example, the following is a kernel of the Normal law:

This method can be used to prove that each of the laws mentioned above satisfies closure under multiplication and to derive its update equations. For each of those laws, in  Table 1  we record its analytic expression, parameters and parameter space, kernel, update equations, and also the formulas specifying the mean and the variance for subsequent reference.

We conclude this section with three comments. First, the update equations show that the combination of a Gamma( 
                         α 
                         , 
                         β 
                      ) and a Gamma( 
                         
                            
                               α 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                         , 
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                      ) is itself a Gamma distribution only if  
                         α 
                         + 
                         
                            
                               α 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                         > 
                         1 
                      . This implies that the closure of the Gamma family is only partial, and the same is true of the Beta family. Second, for any two-parameter law we may consider either the whole two-parameter family, or separate kinds of one-parameter families specified by fixing one parameter and leaving the other free. For example, we may consider the two-parameter family Gamma (in which both parameters  
                         α 
                       and  
                         β 
                       are free), any one-parameter family Gamma 
                         
                            
                            
                               α 
                            
                         
                       (in which  
                         α 
                       is fixed and  
                         β 
                       is free), or any one-parameter family Gamma 
                         
                            
                            
                               β 
                            
                         
                       (in which  
                         α 
                       is free and  
                         β 
                       is fixed). The same may be said of the Beta and the Pareto laws. Third, the probability laws we are considering are all of the exponential type ( Bernardo & Smith, 2000 , p. 198). However, the properties of being of the exponential type and of being closed under multiplication are logically independent of each other. Indeed, there are exponential families that are not closed under multiplication (e.g., the Poisson and Chi-square families) as well as families that are closed under multiplication but are not of the exponential type (the set of all “histograms” on a fixed interval  
                         
                            [ 
                            a 
                            , 
                            b 
                            ] 
                         
                      , each formed of a finite number of columns of positive heights, possibly different widths, and unit total area, is such a family). 
                         1 
                       
                      
                         1 
                         The following characterization can be proved: 
                         An exponential family with canonical representation  
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  | 
                                  θ 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               exp

                         This implies the following corollary: 
                         If an exponential family admits a canonical representation in which  
                               e 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                             for all  
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            ,  
                               θ 
                             is identifiable, and  
                               Θ 
                             is closed under addition, then the family is closed under multiplication. 
                         The five distribution families examined in this study are all of this kind. Furthermore, by applying the “only if” part of the stated characterization, or by direct inspection of the analytic formulas, it can be seen that several prominent distribution families – e.g., Binomial, Negative Binomial, Poisson, Chi-square, Student  
                               t 
                            , Logistic, etc. – are  not  closed under multiplication. Indeed, closure under multiplication turns out to be quite a selective property among the main kinds of distributions discussed in probability theory.

In each separate part of this section we will refer to some parametric family

                               ̃ 
                            
                         
                       – the means implied by the three distributions.

The desirable property to be discussed is the following:  
                         
                            
                               M:  
                               min 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        m 
                                     
                                     
                                        ̇ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                               ≤ 
                               
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̃ 
                                  
                               
                               ≤ 
                               max 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  m 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        m 
                                     
                                     
                                        ̇ 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      This requirement corresponds to an elementary expectation in perceptual psychology: when two factors of a perceptual property do not coincide in the values they support for that property, then researchers may expect that the result of the combined action of the two factors is a compromise between the values supported individually, and thus falls within the interval limited by these values. For example, researchers could formalize such a case by representing the separate effects of the two factors as random variables  
                         X 
                       and  
                         
                            
                               X 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                      , and the combined effect as a weighted average  
                         
                            (5) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̃ 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               c 
                               X 
                               + 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  1 
                                  − 
                                  c 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      This would imply the equation  
                         
                            (6) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̃ 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               c 
                               m 
                               + 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  1 
                                  − 
                                  c 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      which in turn implies the property M for any coefficient  
                         c 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            [ 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ] 
                         
                      , the terms  
                         m 
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                      , and  
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               ̃ 
                            
                         
                       being the means of the random variables involved ( Anderson, 1974 , p. 239;  Dosher, Sperling, & Wurst, 1986 , p. 983;  Jacobs & Fine, 1999 , p. 4066;  Landy, Maloney, Johnston, & Young, 1995 , pp. 395–396).

In the Introduction (Eq.  (2) ) we observed that any Bayesian model based on the Normal law does satisfy Eq.  (6)  with  
                         c 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               τ 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                         ∕ 
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  σ 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            + 
                            
                               
                                  τ 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                      , so that it certainly complies with requirement M. Note that such a model satisfies Eq.  (6)  not because of assumption  (5) , which is extrinsic to the Bayesian construct, but because of the peculiar update equations of the Normal law, as given in  Table 1 . Now we are interested in verifying what would be the behavior, as regards requirement M, of Bayesian models whose prior families correspond to laws other than the Normal one.

                      One of the elementary theoretical expectations alluded to in the Introduction is that the accumulation of stimulus information relevant to some perceptual property ought to reduce the uncertainty and enhance the stability of that property ( Ernst, 2006 , p. 105;  Landy et al., 2011 , p. 18). When set within the frame of our analysis, this expectation means that the variance

                         v 
                       and  
                         
                            
                               v 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                       associated with the separate actions of the factors themselves. The three inequalities introduced above amount to three different ways of formalizing this expectation.

Now we intend to assess how Bayesian models based on probability laws other than the Normal law would behave in relation to these properties.

In particular, part (ii) of the proposition indicates that even the weak desirable property V3 fails to be generally satisfied by Bernoulli distributions, which implies the same for the stronger properties V1 and V2. Furthermore, part (i) signifies that the property V1 holds true whenever the parameter point

Part (i) of  Proposition 8  ensures that the weak property V3 is universally satisfied by Gamma distributions. This is not true of the stronger properties V1 and V2. For example, the assignment

In turn, parts (ii) and (iii) ensure that property V2 is universally satisfied by distributions that belong to the same subfamily Gamma

                      . Beta

                               k 
                            
                         
                      , and of property V2 within any subfamily Pareto 
                         
                            
                            
                               a

                      ; for example, the assignment

                               k 
                            
                         
                       is a conjecture that is not yet disproved. Another elementary expectation in perceptual psychology is that the effect or attractive power of a factor in a perceptual process is stronger when the reliability or precision of the factor itself is higher. When applied to a situation of cue conflict for a perceptual property, this expectation predicts that the most probable value  
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               ̃ 
                            
                         
                       for the perceptual property under the combined action of two stimulus cues is closer to the value  
                         m 
                       favored by one cue or the value  
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                       favored by the other depending on whether the variance  
                         v 
                       associated with the former is lesser than or greater than the variance  
                         
                            
                               v 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                       associated with the latter ( Jacobs, 2002 , p. 346;  Kersten, Mamassian, & Yuille, 2004 , p. 290;  Landy et al., 1995 , p. 396;  Oruç et al., 2003 , p. 2452). Variance is here taken as a measure of unreliability or imprecision.

We consider three possible ways of formalizing this idea. The first is given by this equation:  
                         
                            
                               MV1:  
                               
                                  
                                     | 
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̃ 
                                        
                                     
                                     − 
                                     m 
                                     | 
                                  
                                  
                                     | 
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̃ 
                                        
                                     
                                     − 
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̇ 
                                        
                                     
                                     | 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     v 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           v 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̇ 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      which asserts an equality between the ratio of the distances of  
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               ̃ 
                            
                         
                       from  
                         m 
                       and from  
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                      , and the ratio between the variances. The second is given by this statement:  
                         
                            
                               MV2:  
                               
                                  
                                     | 
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̃ 
                                        
                                     
                                     − 
                                     m 
                                     | 
                                  
                                  
                                     | 
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̃ 
                                        
                                     
                                     − 
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̇ 
                                        
                                     
                                     | 
                                  
                               
                                is an increasing function of  
                               
                                  
                                     v 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           v 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̇ 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      which states that the two ratios are related by monotone functional dependence. The third is expressed by this formula:  
                         
                            
                               MV3:  
                               | 
                               
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̃ 
                                  
                               
                               − 
                               m 
                               | 
                               < 
                               , 
                               = 
                               , 
                               > 
                               | 
                               
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̃ 
                                  
                               
                               − 
                               
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                               | 
                                depending on whether  
                               v 
                               < 
                               , 
                               = 
                               , 
                               > 
                               
                                  
                                     v 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̇ 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      which is tantamount to the biconditional ( 
                         | 
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               ̃ 
                            
                         
                         − 
                         m 
                         | 
                         ∕ 
                         | 
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               ̃ 
                            
                         
                         − 
                         
                            
                               m 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                         | 
                         ≤ 
                         1 
                       iff  
                         v 
                         ∕ 
                         
                            
                               v 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                         ≤ 
                         1 
                      ) concerning the two ratios. Of these desirable properties, the first implies each of the other two, and these two are logically independent of each other, although intuitively the second looks stronger than the third.

Note that if Eqs.  (5) and (11)  are assumed to hold – that is, the weighted average rule of cue integration is presumed, as well as weights that minimize the variance of the resulting effect – then the following equation is predicted:  
                         
                            (18) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     m 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̃ 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     m 
                                     
                                        
                                           v 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̇ 
                                        
                                     
                                     + 
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̇ 
                                        
                                     
                                     v 
                                  
                                  
                                     v 
                                     + 
                                     
                                        
                                           v 
                                        
                                        
                                           ̇ 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      It is easily seen that this equation implies property MV1, and thus also properties MV2 and MV3.

For reasons having to do with update equations (and not with the weighted average rule of cue integration) any two-factor Bayesian model based on the Normal law does indeed satisfy Eq.  (18) . This was noted in the Introduction (Eq.  (2) ). Therefore, this model certainly possesses the properties MV1, MV2, and MV3 expected for the relationship between mean and variance. Then we ask: what is the status of these properties in models that are based on probability laws other than the Normal one?

The main results of this study are summarized in  Table 2 . The letter T in a cell means that the desirable property in the column of the cell has been proved to be generally true for distributions belonging to the family in the row of that cell; the number of the proposition in which this result is established is also specified. The letter F means that the property is generally false within the family; that is, it is falsified by  all  pairs of distributions in the family. The letter A means that there is ambivalence; that is, in the family there are both pairs of distributions that verify the property and pairs that falsify it. Note that properties MV1 and MV3 are never totally false, if only because when a distribution is combined with itself then  
                         v 
                         ∕ 
                         
                            
                               v 
                            
                            
                               ̇

                               m 
                            
                            
                               ̇ 
                            
                         
                         | 
                      , which verifies both MV1 and MV3. Also note that ambivalence and falsity of properties MV1, MV2, and MV3 for one-parameter subfamilies are here specifically stated relatively to the examples Gamma 
                         
                            
                            
                               α 
                               = 
                               5

                      , as illustrated in  Fig. 4, 5 and 6 .

These results were obtained by applying two standard methods of mathematical investigation: formal analysis, for constructing reasoned proofs of the truth of given general statements, and numerical search, for finding possible counterexamples showing the falsity of such statements. In a couple of cases, both methods did not lead to success: the former method, because of the mathematical complexity (e.g., to solve a sixth-degree polynomial inequality on two unknowns), and the latter, because the numerical search (using programs in R here available as online  Appendix A ) did not reveal any counterexample to the statement in question. Those cases correspond to the cells with the symbol T? in the table. That the property in the column of such a cell generally holds true for the distributions in the row is a plausible conjecture, within the limits of our investigation.

The table shows that only the Normal law is endowed with all seven desirable properties discussed in this research. This evidence makes it possible to give the following answer to the question raised in the Introduction: Within the variety of probability laws examined here, the precise fit between theoretical expectations concerning cue integration and implications of the Bayesian modeling is a  peculiarity  of the Normal law. The Geometric and Exponential laws come quite close to that ideal performance, but both fail in the elementary property M concerning the mean. The behavior of the Beta law is comparatively poor, and no improvements appear if it is treated as a one-parameter family, that is, a limitation is imposed consisting of combining Beta distributions that share the same value for one of the two parameters of the law.

A warning that occasionally appears in the literature is that some expected properties of quantitative models of cue integration may lose validity when passing from Normal to non-Normal Bayesian models (e.g.,  Knill, 2007 , p. 17;  Kersten & Mamassian, 2009 , p. 91). This study aimed to put this question under closer scrutiny. It found that there are indeed conspicuous differences in the status of some desirable properties of the mean and variance within non-Normal distribution families that may serve as “prior families” in Bayesian models of cue integration. This reveals the possible limitations of the Bayesian approach when it is extended beyond the mainstream of Normal-based models in perceptual psychology. This conclusion may be mitigated, however, on considering that the desirable properties examined here are only reasonable and generally shared theoretical expectations concerning cue integration. They are not logical necessities. It would be no contradiction, for example, to find experimentally that the joint effect of two stimulus cues is not a weighted mean of the separate effects of them. Thus, in principle, it cannot be excluded that some of the “failures” we have highlighted through formal analysis may on occasions turn out as “successes” at the experimental level.

We discussed five regular types of univariate distributions, whose crucial characteristic, for the purposes of this study, is that of being closed under multiplication, which is a key formal requirement for playing the role of “conjugate prior family” as understood in Bayesian theory. Certainly, the standard probability laws examined do not cover the whole variety of distributions that are or may be of interest in constructing Bayesian models within perceptual or cognitive psychology. There are, for example, models that involve mixed probability distributions in the role of prior and/or likelihood functions, that is, distributions defined as linear combinations of simpler distributions ( Knill, 2007 , pp. 12–13;  Roach, Heron, & McGraw, 2006 , p. 2163); models that are designed not by defining priors and likelihoods in terms of closed mathematical formulas but by constructing them in statistical way, on the basis of large sets of data concerning “statistical properties of the environment” ( Burge, Fowlkes, & Banks, 2010 , p. 7270;  Geisler & Perry, 2009 , pp. 110–111); and problems of perceptual or cognitive inference that may be addressed in terms of “nonparametric Bayesian models”, that is, models theoretically involving large heterogeneous classes of probability distributions that are not susceptible to finite parameterization  (Gershman & Blei, 2012; Gershman, Tenenbaum, & Jäkel, 2016) . Note, however, that the seven “desirable properties” of the mean and variance specified in our study are still meaningful if referred to this wider methodological scenario. Furthermore, the variegated results found through our analysis naturally suggest questions about the status of those desirable properties under distributions that are freer and more complex than the standard types we have examined.

Lastly, shifting the focus from perceptual or cognitive psychology to statistical theory and psychometrics, a salient feature of the Bayesian framework is the idea of information integration in terms of the iterated updating of probability distributions. One starts with a “prior distribution”  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       on a parameter space  
                         X 
                      . Through combination (performed according to the Bayes rule) with a “likelihood function”  
                         
                            
                               L 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       that represents a piece of empirical information, the prior  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       updates into a “posterior distribution”  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       on the space  
                         X 
                      . Through combination with another likelihood function  
                         
                            
                               L 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       that represents a fresh piece of empirical information, the posterior  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       updates into a new posterior distribution  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                      , again on the space  
                         X 
                      . And so on, according to the saying, “Today’s posterior is tomorrow’s prior” ( Lindley, 1972 , p. 2). A natural requirement for the usefulness of this process is that the precision of the distributions established step by step on the space  
                         X 
                       is increasing; that is, along the series  
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                         , 
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         , 
                         
                            
                               P 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                         , 
                         … 
                       of updated distributions, the variance is decreasing (“consistency of Bayesian estimation”:  Diaconis & Freedman, 1986 ;  Ghosh, Delampady, & Samanta, 2006 , p. 100; “convergence in Bayesian adaptive estimation”:  Kontsevich & Tyler, 1999 , p. 2733;  Watson & Pelli, 1983 , p. 118). This requirement is strictly related to the problem addressed in Section  4 , concerning the desirable properties of the variance. The results we obtained could, therefore, also be meaningful in this perspective, which refers to Bayesian statistical and psychometric methods.
